question:
Suppose $p$ is an odd prime. Prove that
\[
\sum_{j=0}^p \binom{p}{j} \binom{p+j}{j} \equiv 2^p + 1\pmod{p^2}.
\]
solution:
Solution 1. The sum \( \sum_{j=0}^{p}\binom{p}{j}\binom{p+j}{j} \) is the coefficient of \( x^{p} \) in
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j=0}^{p}\binom{p}{j}(1+x)^{p+j} & =\left(\sum_{j=0}^{p}\binom{p}{j}(1+x)^{j}\right)(1+x)^{p} \\
& =((1+x)+1)^{p}(1+x)^{p} \\
& =(2+x)^{p}(1+x)^{p}
\end{aligned}
\]
so this coefficient equals
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{p}\binom{p}{k}\binom{p}{p-k} 2^{k}
\]

But \( p \) divides \( \binom{p}{k} \) for \( k \neq 0 \). Thus,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j=0}^{p}\binom{p}{j}\binom{p+j}{p} & \equiv\binom{p}{0}\binom{p}{p} 2^{0}+\binom{p}{p}\binom{p}{0} 2^{p} \\
& \equiv 1+2^{p} \quad\left(\bmod p^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

Solution 2. Modulo \( p \), the sets \( \{1,2, \ldots, p-1\} \) and \( \left\{1^{-1}, 2^{-1}, \ldots,(p-1)^{-1}\right\} \) are the same (where the inverses are modulo \( p \) ). Thus
\[
\begin{aligned}
\binom{2 p}{p} & =\frac{p+1}{1} \cdot \frac{p+2}{2} \cdots \frac{p+(p-1)}{p-1} \cdot \frac{2 p}{p} \\
& \equiv\left(1^{-1} p+1\right)\left(2^{-1} p+1\right) \cdots\left((p-1)^{-1} p+1\right) \cdot 2 \\
& \equiv 2(1 p+1)(2 p+1) \cdots((p-1) p+1) \\
& \equiv 2\left(\left(\sum_{k=1}^{p-1} k\right) p+1\right) \\
& \equiv 2 \quad\left(\bmod p^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

Although \( 1^{-1}, \ldots,(p-1)^{-1} \) are only defined modulo \( p \), they appear in the equations above multiplied by \( p \), so the terms make sense modulo \( p^{2} \).
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j=0}^{p}\binom{p}{j}\binom{p+j}{j} & =1+\sum_{j=1}^{p-1}\binom{p}{j} \frac{p+1}{1} \frac{p+2}{2} \cdots \frac{p+j}{j}+\binom{2 p}{p} \\
& \equiv 1+\sum_{j=1}^{p-1}\binom{p}{j}\left(1^{-1} p+1\right)\left(2^{-1} p+1\right) \cdots\left(j^{-1} p+1\right)+2 \\
& \equiv 1+\sum_{j=1}^{p-1}\binom{p}{j}+2 \quad\left(\text { since } p \left\lvert\,\binom{ p}{j}\right. \text { for } 1 \leq j \leq p-1\right) \\
& \equiv \sum_{j=0}^{p}\binom{p}{j}+1 \\
& \equiv 2^{p}+1 \quad\left(\bmod p^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

descriptive_long.question:
Suppose $primeodd$ is an odd prime. Prove that
\[
\sum_{iterator=0}^{primeodd} \binom{primeodd}{iterator} \binom{primeodd+iterator}{iterator} \equiv 2^{primeodd} + 1\pmod{primeodd^{2}}.
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution 1. The sum \( \sum_{iterator=0}^{primeodd}\binom{primeodd}{iterator}\binom{primeodd+iterator}{iterator} \) is the coefficient of \( placeholder^{primeodd} \) in
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{iterator=0}^{primeodd}\binom{primeodd}{iterator}(1+placeholder)^{primeodd+iterator} & =\left(\sum_{iterator=0}^{primeodd}\binom{primeodd}{iterator}(1+placeholder)^{iterator}\right)(1+placeholder)^{primeodd} \\
& =((1+placeholder)+1)^{primeodd}(1+placeholder)^{primeodd} \\
& =(2+placeholder)^{primeodd}(1+placeholder)^{primeodd}
\end{aligned}
\]
so this coefficient equals
\[
\sum_{indextwo=0}^{primeodd}\binom{primeodd}{indextwo}\binom{primeodd}{primeodd-indextwo} 2^{indextwo}
\]

But \( primeodd \) divides \( \binom{primeodd}{indextwo} \) for \( indextwo \neq 0 \). Thus,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{iterator=0}^{primeodd}\binom{primeodd}{iterator}\binom{primeodd+iterator}{primeodd} & \equiv\binom{primeodd}{0}\binom{primeodd}{primeodd} 2^{0}+\binom{primeodd}{primeodd}\binom{primeodd}{0} 2^{primeodd} \\
& \equiv 1+2^{primeodd} \quad\left(\bmod primeodd^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

Solution 2. Modulo \( primeodd \), the sets \( \{1,2, \ldots, primeodd-1\} \) and \( \left\{1^{-1}, 2^{-1}, \ldots,(primeodd-1)^{-1}\right\} \) are the same (where the inverses are modulo \( primeodd \) ). Thus
\[
\begin{aligned}
\binom{2\,primeodd}{primeodd} & =\frac{primeodd+1}{1} \cdot \frac{primeodd+2}{2} \cdots \frac{primeodd+(primeodd-1)}{primeodd-1} \cdot \frac{2\,primeodd}{primeodd} \\
& \equiv\left(1^{-1} primeodd+1\right)\left(2^{-1} primeodd+1\right) \cdots\left((primeodd-1)^{-1} primeodd+1\right) \cdot 2 \\
& \equiv 2(1\,primeodd+1)(2\,primeodd+1) \cdots((primeodd-1)\,primeodd+1) \\
& \equiv 2\left(\left(\sum_{indextwo=1}^{primeodd-1} indextwo\right) primeodd+1\right) \\
& \equiv 2 \quad\left(\bmod primeodd^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

Although \( 1^{-1}, \ldots,(primeodd-1)^{-1} \) are only defined modulo \( primeodd \), they appear in the equations above multiplied by \( primeodd \), so the terms make sense modulo \( primeodd^{2} \).
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{iterator=0}^{primeodd}\binom{primeodd}{iterator}\binom{primeodd+iterator}{iterator} & =1+\sum_{iterator=1}^{primeodd-1}\binom{primeodd}{iterator} \frac{primeodd+1}{1} \frac{primeodd+2}{2} \cdots \frac{primeodd+iterator}{iterator}+\binom{2\,primeodd}{primeodd} \\
& \equiv 1+\sum_{iterator=1}^{primeodd-1}\binom{primeodd}{iterator}\left(1^{-1} primeodd+1\right)\left(2^{-1} primeodd+1\right) \cdots\left(iterator^{-1} primeodd+1\right)+2 \\
& \equiv 1+\sum_{iterator=1}^{primeodd-1}\binom{primeodd}{iterator}+2 \quad\left(\text { since } primeodd \left\lvert\,\binom{ primeodd}{iterator}\right.\text { for } 1 \leq iterator \leq primeodd-1\right) \\
& \equiv \sum_{iterator=0}^{primeodd}\binom{primeodd}{iterator}+1 \\
& \equiv 2^{primeodd}+1 \quad\left(\bmod primeodd^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Suppose $evergreen$ is an odd prime. Prove that
\[
\sum_{rainstorm=0}^{evergreen} \binom{evergreen}{rainstorm} \binom{evergreen+rainstorm}{rainstorm} \equiv 2^{evergreen} + 1\pmod{evergreen^2}.
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution 1. The sum \( \sum_{rainstorm=0}^{evergreen}\binom{evergreen}{rainstorm}\binom{evergreen+rainstorm}{rainstorm} \) is the coefficient of \( lighthouse^{evergreen} \) in
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{rainstorm=0}^{evergreen}\binom{evergreen}{rainstorm}(1+lighthouse)^{evergreen+rainstorm} & =\left(\sum_{rainstorm=0}^{evergreen}\binom{evergreen}{rainstorm}(1+lighthouse)^{rainstorm}\right)(1+lighthouse)^{evergreen} \\
& =((1+lighthouse)+1)^{evergreen}(1+lighthouse)^{evergreen} \\
& =(2+lighthouse)^{evergreen}(1+lighthouse)^{evergreen}
\end{aligned}
\]
so this coefficient equals
\[
\sum_{sandstone=0}^{evergreen}\binom{evergreen}{sandstone}\binom{evergreen}{evergreen-sandstone} 2^{sandstone}
\]

But \( evergreen \) divides \( \binom{evergreen}{sandstone} \) for \( sandstone \neq 0 \). Thus,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{rainstorm=0}^{evergreen}\binom{evergreen}{rainstorm}\binom{evergreen+rainstorm}{evergreen} & \equiv\binom{evergreen}{0}\binom{evergreen}{evergreen} 2^{0}+\binom{evergreen}{evergreen}\binom{evergreen}{0} 2^{evergreen} \\
& \equiv 1+2^{evergreen} \quad\left(\bmod evergreen^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

Solution 2. Modulo \( evergreen \), the sets \( \{1,2, \ldots, evergreen-1\} \) and \( \left\{1^{-1}, 2^{-1}, \ldots,(evergreen-1)^{-1}\right\} \) are the same (where the inverses are modulo \( evergreen \) ). Thus
\[
\begin{aligned}
\binom{2 evergreen}{evergreen} & =\frac{evergreen+1}{1} \cdot \frac{evergreen+2}{2} \cdots \frac{evergreen+(evergreen-1)}{evergreen-1} \cdot \frac{2 evergreen}{evergreen} \\
& \equiv\left(1^{-1} evergreen+1\right)\left(2^{-1} evergreen+1\right) \cdots\left((evergreen-1)^{-1} evergreen+1\right) \cdot 2 \\
& \equiv 2(1 evergreen+1)(2 evergreen+1) \cdots((evergreen-1) evergreen+1) \\
& \equiv 2\left(\left(\sum_{sandstone=1}^{evergreen-1} sandstone\right) evergreen+1\right) \\
& \equiv 2 \quad\left(\bmod evergreen^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

Although \( 1^{-1}, \ldots,(evergreen-1)^{-1} \) are only defined modulo \( evergreen \), they appear in the equations above multiplied by \( evergreen \), so the terms make sense modulo \( evergreen^{2} \).
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{rainstorm=0}^{evergreen}\binom{evergreen}{rainstorm}\binom{evergreen+rainstorm}{rainstorm} & =1+\sum_{rainstorm=1}^{evergreen-1}\binom{evergreen}{rainstorm} \frac{evergreen+1}{1} \frac{evergreen+2}{2} \cdots \frac{evergreen+rainstorm}{rainstorm}+\binom{2 evergreen}{evergreen} \\
& \equiv 1+\sum_{rainstorm=1}^{evergreen-1}\binom{evergreen}{rainstorm}\left(1^{-1} evergreen+1\right)\left(2^{-1} evergreen+1\right) \cdots\left(rainstorm^{-1} evergreen+1\right)+2 \\
& \equiv 1+\sum_{rainstorm=1}^{evergreen-1}\binom{evergreen}{rainstorm}+2 \quad\left(\text { since } evergreen \left\lvert\,\binom{ evergreen}{rainstorm}\right. \text { for } 1 \leq rainstorm \leq evergreen-1\right) \\
& \equiv \sum_{rainstorm=0}^{evergreen}\binom{evergreen}{rainstorm}+1 \\
& \equiv 2^{evergreen}+1 \quad\left(\bmod evergreen^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Suppose $composite$ is an odd prime. Prove that
\[
\sum_{aggregate=0}^{composite} \binom{composite}{aggregate} \binom{composite+aggregate}{aggregate} \equiv 2^{composite} + 1\pmod{composite^{2}}.
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution 1. The sum \( \sum_{aggregate=0}^{composite}\binom{composite}{aggregate}\binom{composite+aggregate}{aggregate} \) is the coefficient of \( immutable^{composite} \) in
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{aggregate=0}^{composite}\binom{composite}{aggregate}(1+immutable)^{composite+aggregate} & =\left(\sum_{aggregate=0}^{composite}\binom{composite}{aggregate}(1+immutable)^{aggregate}\right)(1+immutable)^{composite} \\
& =((1+immutable)+1)^{composite}(1+immutable)^{composite} \\
& =(2+immutable)^{composite}(1+immutable)^{composite}
\end{aligned}
\]
so this coefficient equals
\[
\sum_{totality=0}^{composite}\binom{composite}{totality}\binom{composite}{composite-totality} 2^{totality}
\]

But \( composite \) divides \( \binom{composite}{totality} \) for \( totality \neq 0 \). Thus,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{aggregate=0}^{composite}\binom{composite}{aggregate}\binom{composite+aggregate}{composite} & \equiv\binom{composite}{0}\binom{composite}{composite} 2^{0}+\binom{composite}{composite}\binom{composite}{0} 2^{composite} \\
& \equiv 1+2^{composite} \quad\left(\bmod composite^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

Solution 2. Modulo \( composite \), the sets \( \{1,2, \ldots, composite-1\} \) and \( \left\{1^{-1}, 2^{-1}, \ldots,(composite-1)^{-1}\right\} \) are the same (where the inverses are modulo \( composite \) ). Thus
\[
\begin{aligned}
\binom{2 composite}{composite} & =\frac{composite+1}{1} \cdot \frac{composite+2}{2} \cdots \frac{composite+(composite-1)}{composite-1} \cdot \frac{2 composite}{composite} \\
& \equiv\left(1^{-1} composite+1\right)\left(2^{-1} composite+1\right) \cdots\left((composite-1)^{-1} composite+1\right) \cdot 2 \\
& \equiv 2(1 composite+1)(2 composite+1) \cdots((composite-1) composite+1) \\
& \equiv 2\left(\left(\sum_{totality=1}^{composite-1} totality\right) composite+1\right) \\
& \equiv 2 \quad\left(\bmod composite^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

Although \( 1^{-1}, \ldots,(composite-1)^{-1} \) are only defined modulo \( composite \), they appear in the equations above multiplied by \( composite \), so the terms make sense modulo \( composite^{2} \).
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{aggregate=0}^{composite}\binom{composite}{aggregate}\binom{composite+aggregate}{aggregate} & =1+\sum_{aggregate=1}^{composite-1}\binom{composite}{aggregate} \frac{composite+1}{1} \frac{composite+2}{2} \cdots \frac{composite+aggregate}{aggregate}+\binom{2 composite}{composite} \\
& \equiv 1+\sum_{aggregate=1}^{composite-1}\binom{composite}{aggregate}\left(1^{-1} composite+1\right)\left(2^{-1} composite+1\right) \cdots\left(aggregate^{-1} composite+1\right)+2 \\
& \equiv 1+\sum_{aggregate=1}^{composite-1}\binom{composite}{aggregate}+2 \quad\left(\text { since } composite \left\lvert\,\binom{ composite}{aggregate}\right. \text { for } 1 \leq aggregate \leq composite-1\right) \\
& \equiv \sum_{aggregate=0}^{composite}\binom{composite}{aggregate}+1 \\
& \equiv 2^{composite}+1 \quad\left(\bmod composite^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

garbled_string.question:
Suppose $hbdksewq$ is an odd prime. Prove that
\[
\sum_{mrlgcaue=0}^{hbdksewq} \binom{hbdksewq}{mrlgcaue} \binom{hbdksewq+mrlgcaue}{mrlgcaue} \equiv 2^{hbdksewq} + 1\pmod{hbdksewq^2}.
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution 1. The sum \( \sum_{mrlgcaue=0}^{hbdksewq}\binom{hbdksewq}{mrlgcaue}\binom{hbdksewq+mrlgcaue}{mrlgcaue} \) is the coefficient of \( qzxwvtnp^{hbdksewq} \) in
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{mrlgcaue=0}^{hbdksewq}\binom{hbdksewq}{mrlgcaue}(1+qzxwvtnp)^{hbdksewq+mrlgcaue} & =\left(\sum_{mrlgcaue=0}^{hbdksewq}\binom{hbdksewq}{mrlgcaue}(1+qzxwvtnp)^{mrlgcaue}\right)(1+qzxwvtnp)^{hbdksewq} \\
& =((1+qzxwvtnp)+1)^{hbdksewq}(1+qzxwvtnp)^{hbdksewq} \\
& =(2+qzxwvtnp)^{hbdksewq}(1+qzxwvtnp)^{hbdksewq}
\end{aligned}
\]
so this coefficient equals
\[
\sum_{tpsvznqx=0}^{hbdksewq}\binom{hbdksewq}{tpsvznqx}\binom{hbdksewq}{hbdksewq-tpsvznqx} 2^{tpsvznqx}
\]

But \( hbdksewq \) divides \( \binom{hbdksewq}{tpsvznqx} \) for \( tpsvznqx \neq 0 \). Thus,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{mrlgcaue=0}^{hbdksewq}\binom{hbdksewq}{mrlgcaue}\binom{hbdksewq+mrlgcaue}{hbdksewq} & \equiv\binom{hbdksewq}{0}\binom{hbdksewq}{hbdksewq} 2^{0}+\binom{hbdksewq}{hbdksewq}\binom{hbdksewq}{0} 2^{hbdksewq} \\
& \equiv 1+2^{hbdksewq} \quad\left(\bmod hbdksewq^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

Solution 2. Modulo \( hbdksewq \), the sets \( \{1,2, \ldots, hbdksewq-1\} \) and \( \{1^{-1}, 2^{-1}, \ldots,(hbdksewq-1)^{-1}\} \) are the same (where the inverses are modulo \( hbdksewq \) ). Thus
\[
\begin{aligned}
\binom{2 hbdksewq}{hbdksewq} & =\frac{hbdksewq+1}{1} \cdot \frac{hbdksewq+2}{2} \cdots \frac{hbdksewq+(hbdksewq-1)}{hbdksewq-1} \cdot \frac{2 hbdksewq}{hbdksewq} \\
& \equiv\left(1^{-1} hbdksewq+1\right)\left(2^{-1} hbdksewq+1\right) \cdots\left((hbdksewq-1)^{-1} hbdksewq+1\right) \cdot 2 \\
& \equiv 2(1 hbdksewq+1)(2 hbdksewq+1) \cdots((hbdksewq-1) hbdksewq+1) \\
& \equiv 2\left(\left(\sum_{tpsvznqx=1}^{hbdksewq-1} tpsvznqx\right) hbdksewq+1\right) \\
& \equiv 2 \quad\left(\bmod hbdksewq^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

Although \( 1^{-1}, \ldots,(hbdksewq-1)^{-1} \) are only defined modulo \( hbdksewq \), they appear in the equations above multiplied by \( hbdksewq \), so the terms make sense modulo \( hbdksewq^{2} \).
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{mrlgcaue=0}^{hbdksewq}\binom{hbdksewq}{mrlgcaue}\binom{hbdksewq+mrlgcaue}{mrlgcaue} & =1+\sum_{mrlgcaue=1}^{hbdksewq-1}\binom{hbdksewq}{mrlgcaue} \frac{hbdksewq+1}{1} \frac{hbdksewq+2}{2} \cdots \frac{hbdksewq+mrlgcaue}{mrlgcaue}+\binom{2 hbdksewq}{hbdksewq} \\
& \equiv 1+\sum_{mrlgcaue=1}^{hbdksewq-1}\binom{hbdksewq}{mrlgcaue}\left(1^{-1} hbdksewq+1\right)\left(2^{-1} hbdksewq+1\right) \cdots\left(mrlgcaue^{-1} hbdksewq+1\right)+2 \\
& \equiv 1+\sum_{mrlgcaue=1}^{hbdksewq-1}\binom{hbdksewq}{mrlgcaue}+2 \quad\left(\text { since } hbdksewq \left\lvert\,\binom{ hbdksewq}{mrlgcaue}\right. \text { for } 1 \leq mrlgcaue \leq hbdksewq-1\right) \\
& \equiv \sum_{mrlgcaue=0}^{hbdksewq}\binom{hbdksewq}{mrlgcaue}+1 \\
& \equiv 2^{hbdksewq}+1 \quad\left(\bmod hbdksewq^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let p be an odd prime and define  
  S(p)=  \sum _{j=0}^{p}  \sum _{k=0}^{p}  2^{\,j}\,3^{\,k}\,
            \binom{p}{j}\,\binom{p}{k}\,\binom{p+j+k}{\,p}.  
Prove the super-congruence  
  S(p)  \equiv   8^{\,p}+9^{\,p}+12^{\,p}\pmod{p^{2}}.

(The congruence is to hold for every odd prime p, including p=3.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution: Throughout ``\equiv '' means congruence in the ring \mathbb{Z}/p^2\mathbb{Z}; every quantity we reduce will first be shown to be an integer. Step 1. A generating-function translation For an indeterminate z we have binom{p+j+k}{p}=[z^{p}](1+z)^{p+j+k}. Therefore S(p)=\sum _{j,k=0}^{p}2^{j}3^{k}\binom{p}{j}\binom{p}{k} [z^{p}](1+z)^{p+j+k} (1) =[z^{p}](1+z)^{p}\Bigl(\sum _{j=0}^{p}2^{j}\binom{p}{j}(1+z)^{j}\Bigr) \Bigl(\sum _{k=0}^{p}3^{k}\binom{p}{k}(1+z)^{k}\Bigr). The two inner sums are binomial expansions: \sum _{j=0}^{p}2^{j}\binom{p}{j}(1+z)^{j}=(3+2z)^{p}, \sum _{k=0}^{p}3^{k}\binom{p}{k}(1+z)^{k}=(4+3z)^{p}. Hence S(p)= [z^{p}] (1+z)^{p}(3+2z)^{p}(4+3z)^{p}. (2) Step 2. Expanding without introducing denominators Write each factor in ordinary binomial form: (3+2z)^{p}=\sum _{a=0}^{p}\binom{p}{a}3^{\,p-a}(2z)^{a}, (4+3z)^{p}=\sum _{b=0}^{p}\binom{p}{b}4^{\,p-b}(3z)^{b}, (1+z)^{p}=\sum _{c=0}^{p}\binom{p}{c}z^{c}. Collecting the coefficient of z^{p} gives S(p)=\sum _{a+b+c=p}\binom{p}{a}\binom{p}{b}\binom{p}{c} 3^{\,p-a}4^{\,p-b}2^{\,a}3^{\,b}. (3) Step 3. Isolating a big constant factor Factor 12^{p}=3^{p}4^{p} out of every summand: S(p)=12^{p}\cdot C(p), where (4) C(p)=\sum _{a+b+c=p}\binom{p}{a}\binom{p}{b}\binom{p}{c} \alpha ^{\,a}\beta ^{\,b}, \alpha =2/3, \beta =3/4. (5) Because 12^{p}\cdot \alpha ^{a}\beta ^{b}=3^{\,p-a+b}2^{\,a}4^{\,p-b} is an integer, equation (4) shows that S(p) itself is an integer: denominators have been cleared before any modular reduction. Step 4. A p-adic divisibility lemma For 0

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Double‐sum structure:  The problem now involves a genuinely two-dimensional
   summation; separating and handling the inner sum demands an additional layer
   of organisation compared with the single index in the original tasks.

2. Sophisticated generating functions:  Extracting T_j required transforming
   a binomial sum into a coefficient-of-power problem and manipulating two
   simultaneous binomial expansions, rather than the lone Vandermonde step of
   the originals.

3. Careful p-adic bookkeeping:  Keeping only the i=0 and i=p terms in (2)
   necessitates explicit p-adic divisibility arguments inside a nested
   expansion.  Such p-adic filtration does not appear in the earlier variants.

4. Re-use and fusion of results:  The solution must recognise and embed the
   previously derived congruence (the current kernel variant) as a sub-routine,
   showing inter-dependence of multiple advanced ideas.

5. Exponent arithmetic:  Combining 4^{p} with 2^{p} and 3^{p} to obtain
   8^{p} and 12^{p} is simple numerically but subtle modulo p², and the
   justifications rely on multiplicative properties inside the modulus.

Altogether these additions create a problem that is substantially deeper and
less susceptible to straightforward pattern matching than either the original
or the current kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let p be an odd prime and define  
  S(p)=  \sum _{j=0}^{p}  \sum _{k=0}^{p}  2^{\,j}\,3^{\,k}\,
            \binom{p}{j}\,\binom{p}{k}\,\binom{p+j+k}{\,p}.  
Prove the super-congruence  
  S(p)  \equiv   8^{\,p}+9^{\,p}+12^{\,p}\pmod{p^{2}}.

(The congruence is to hold for every odd prime p, including p=3.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution: Throughout ``\equiv '' means congruence in the ring \mathbb{Z}/p^2\mathbb{Z}; every quantity we reduce will first be shown to be an integer. Step 1. A generating-function translation For an indeterminate z we have binom{p+j+k}{p}=[z^{p}](1+z)^{p+j+k}. Therefore S(p)=\sum _{j,k=0}^{p}2^{j}3^{k}\binom{p}{j}\binom{p}{k} [z^{p}](1+z)^{p+j+k} (1) =[z^{p}](1+z)^{p}\Bigl(\sum _{j=0}^{p}2^{j}\binom{p}{j}(1+z)^{j}\Bigr) \Bigl(\sum _{k=0}^{p}3^{k}\binom{p}{k}(1+z)^{k}\Bigr). The two inner sums are binomial expansions: \sum _{j=0}^{p}2^{j}\binom{p}{j}(1+z)^{j}=(3+2z)^{p}, \sum _{k=0}^{p}3^{k}\binom{p}{k}(1+z)^{k}=(4+3z)^{p}. Hence S(p)= [z^{p}] (1+z)^{p}(3+2z)^{p}(4+3z)^{p}. (2) Step 2. Expanding without introducing denominators Write each factor in ordinary binomial form: (3+2z)^{p}=\sum _{a=0}^{p}\binom{p}{a}3^{\,p-a}(2z)^{a}, (4+3z)^{p}=\sum _{b=0}^{p}\binom{p}{b}4^{\,p-b}(3z)^{b}, (1+z)^{p}=\sum _{c=0}^{p}\binom{p}{c}z^{c}. Collecting the coefficient of z^{p} gives S(p)=\sum _{a+b+c=p}\binom{p}{a}\binom{p}{b}\binom{p}{c} 3^{\,p-a}4^{\,p-b}2^{\,a}3^{\,b}. (3) Step 3. Isolating a big constant factor Factor 12^{p}=3^{p}4^{p} out of every summand: S(p)=12^{p}\cdot C(p), where (4) C(p)=\sum _{a+b+c=p}\binom{p}{a}\binom{p}{b}\binom{p}{c} \alpha ^{\,a}\beta ^{\,b}, \alpha =2/3, \beta =3/4. (5) Because 12^{p}\cdot \alpha ^{a}\beta ^{b}=3^{\,p-a+b}2^{\,a}4^{\,p-b} is an integer, equation (4) shows that S(p) itself is an integer: denominators have been cleared before any modular reduction. Step 4. A p-adic divisibility lemma For 0

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Double‐sum structure:  The problem now involves a genuinely two-dimensional
   summation; separating and handling the inner sum demands an additional layer
   of organisation compared with the single index in the original tasks.

2. Sophisticated generating functions:  Extracting T_j required transforming
   a binomial sum into a coefficient-of-power problem and manipulating two
   simultaneous binomial expansions, rather than the lone Vandermonde step of
   the originals.

3. Careful p-adic bookkeeping:  Keeping only the i=0 and i=p terms in (2)
   necessitates explicit p-adic divisibility arguments inside a nested
   expansion.  Such p-adic filtration does not appear in the earlier variants.

4. Re-use and fusion of results:  The solution must recognise and embed the
   previously derived congruence (the current kernel variant) as a sub-routine,
   showing inter-dependence of multiple advanced ideas.

5. Exponent arithmetic:  Combining 4^{p} with 2^{p} and 3^{p} to obtain
   8^{p} and 12^{p} is simple numerically but subtle modulo p², and the
   justifications rely on multiplicative properties inside the modulus.

Altogether these additions create a problem that is substantially deeper and
less susceptible to straightforward pattern matching than either the original
or the current kernel variant.
